column1: Sentence
VPN MODULE AND BRIDGE
Technical Specifications
GLOSSARY.
Point - In a Peer-to-Peer connection, one of the connection points.
Local - the one closer to us; remote - the one farther away.
Bridge - In this context, a bridge is an intermediary server, or backconnect server, that switches VPN connections between each other.
PURPOSE
To develop a complex, similar in functions to TeamViewer in VPN mode, consisting of three parts:
- A module on machine A, to which we want to connect (far end);
- A VPN client on machine B, from which we want to connect to machine A and its entire local network (the local end);
- VPN bridge B, which accepts incoming VPN connections from A and B and routes them between them (router, hub).
The point of using a VPN bridge is that machine A is usually behind a NAT/firewall and cannot be directly connected to it.
And also because incoming and outgoing TCP ports may be closed, so you need to use ports like 80 and 443 for outgoing connections.
for outgoing connections.
So the connection works like this:
- A connects to B
- B connects to C
- B forwards routes between A's and B's local points so that B can see A and its network, but not vice versa.
The emphasis here is not so much on VPN as on routing.
You need an AMAZING way to join a remote network.
But a VPN is also needed, because unencrypted traffic can be cut off by firewalls and DPI filters.
MODULE: IMPLEMENTATION
The module is implemented according to the rules from the "module_HOWTO" document.
The module has a single config named "vpnsrv".
The content of the config is plain text, line separator \r\n.
The line contains one address/server name in the format address:port or hostname:port.
This is the address of VPN bridge B.
It is assumed that the module is running with administrator rights.
At startup, the module acts as follows:
- waits until the config with bridge addresses is received
- randomly searches for a working bridge (to which it managed to open a connection)
- if there are no working bridges, issues the "VPN bridge failure" event and then requests its own unloading by the "WantRelease" event.
- creates a new VPN connection to bridge B using standard Windows tools ("Adapter and Sharing Center" - "Connect to network").
The login to the connection is ParentID
(see ParentInfo in "module_HOWTO"), the password is ParentGroup.
- starts the VPN connection to bridge B
- periodically checks the connection status (you can simply ping to the address of remote point A)
- when the connection is closed, restores it
- when unloading the module by calling Release(), terminates the connection and removes it from the system.
In general, it is suggested to use the built-in Windows VPN-client.
But we need to find a way to suppress the balloons that pop up
from the system tray when connecting/disconnecting.
But here the decision is up to the developer.
The standard client is offered because it is already in the OS; if there is a way to create a lightweight VPN-client,
that takes up to 1 megabyte and is a single .dll
(together with a module), this is also an acceptable option.
BRIDGE: IMPLEMENTATION
Presumably, it is possible to organize a bridge on OpenVPN.
Routing raises questions.
It is very likely that machines A from different organizations will have repeated subnet addresses (192.168.0.x, 192.168.1.x, 10.0.x.x, etc.).
The same is possible for clients B behind NAT.
Therefore, if two or more independent clients (B1, B2) and modules (A1, A2) connect to the bridge at the same time, there is a high probability of collision in routing between subnets.
The naive solution is to deny more than one A/B pair.
We need to find ways around this problem.
Assume that there is an HTTP API to manage the bridge other than OpenVPN.
The API has the following call:
GET /api/session/ParentID/ParentGroup HTTP/1.1
Here ParentID/ParentGroup are the identifiers (IDs) of module A.
In response, the bridge sends in plain text the OpenVPN client config for client B, which can be used to connect to its paired module A.
When processing the request, the bridge:
- remembers the ID of module A and stores the ID as a valid login/password with a lifetime of 3600 seconds (customization)
- generates a session login/password for paired client B with a lifetime of 3600 seconds (customization)
- generates an OpenVPN config for paired client B and delivers it along with HTTP code 200.
- if it is impossible to generate an OpenVPN config due to insufficient data (for example, the subnet address of module A is unknown and it is impossible to force a route to its subnet),
the bridge remembers the request and gives its number (receipt) to the client, until the data is clarified, together with HTTP code 202 Accepted
- The HTTP client periodically polls the readiness of the request with the same URI GET /api/session/ParentID/ParentGroup HTTP/1.1
and analyzes the response code:
200 - response ready
204 - in processing
40* - processing error
HTTP API should be protected by simple protection against unauthorized access - most likely Security by obscurity - a complex non-indexed path to API,
or HTTP Basic Auth.
Thus, the bridge carries the following functions:
1. module authorization
- The module must specify ParentID/ParentGroup as login and password for the connection; the bridge must authorize the module.
The bridge receives the list of actual logins/passwords and their lifetime in seconds via HTTP API.
Only connections with passwords from this list can be authorized.
2. client authorization
- for the client, the bridge generates session login/password and client config for OpenVPN and gives them through the control API.
USER SCENARIO
I, as a user, went into the dark admin, selected a client session with the CONCRETE ID and group I want to work on behalf of.
I clicked the VPN button in the panel.
Behind the scenes, the VPN module on the far end (A) started up, got a config with the bridge address and brought up a VPN connection to it, LOGGED in with my ID and group.
This way the bridge knows who is connected to it - it needs it to search for connection pairs A and B.
Meanwhile, the dark admin pulled the bridge's web backend over the REST API, requesting a config for the connection just raised.
Specifying the bot ID and group at that.
The bridge generated a session login-password for the paired client VPN connection, and generated a temporary OpenVPN config (plain text file)
for the paired connection.
And gave it in response to a request via the REST API.
The dark admin gives the config to the user.
The user receives this config and slips it to the OpenVPN client.
A paired connection is brought up from the user side to the bridge.
The bridge can bridge a pair of connections - the end (A-B) and the user (B-B) - because it knows both the bot ID and group,
identifying A-B, and the user's session password identifying B-B. The bridge checks the route in the OS routing table.
Additional routes for the far ends of the connection pair have already been given when the corresponding connections are established.
MODULE USAGE INSTRUCTIONS
1. Go to lero, select the session we are interested in
2. Execute
push_back
command code 62
command parameters
vpnDllstart
3. wait for the module to start (look in the lero of messages from the module)
2020-01-16 13:25:58 vpnDll net flyAgaric Can't connect to 173.232.146.30, error 718
2020-01-16 13:25:59 vpnDll net flyAgaric VPN bridge failure
The testing algorithm is as follows
1. run the software on the test machine, let it deploy
2. open its page in lero admin.
3. run the command push back: 62 vpnDll start
4. wait until the module reacts and see in the admin log how it works.
at the same time watch what happens on the screen.
well and look for the session opened by the module in the admin http:///admin/index.php?r=vpn%2Findex.
in short, a simple test - the module works if you start it in lero with the command 62 vpnDll start and get a session in the vpn admin.
